 {"id":"ntm-0q9o","title":"[EPIC] Robot Command Reliability","description":"# Epic: Robot Command Reliability

## Goal
Make robot commands bulletproof for AI agent consumption. This is the CRITICAL PATH for AI coding agents using NTM programmatically.

## Background
NTM serves two user personas: human operators (TUI) and AI coding agents (robot commands). AI agents are programmatic consumers - they parse JSON, check exit codes, and make decisions based on command output. Any inconsistency or ambiguity causes bugs in agent workflows.

Currently, robot commands have several reliability issues:
1. **Timeout units are inconsistent** - spawn uses seconds, others use milliseconds
2. **Error output location varies** - some to stderr, some to stdout JSON
3. **Agent type naming differs** - cc vs claude vs claude_code
4. **Unimplemented features return errors** - breaks agent error handling

## Success Criteria
- AI agents can reliably parse ALL robot command output
- No ambiguity in timeout values, error locations, or agent naming
- Unimplemented features don't break agent workflows
- All changes are backwards compatible with deprecation warnings

## Beads in this Epic
- R1: Duration string support for timeouts
- R2: Standardized JSON error responses
- R3: Agent type aliasing
- R4: Graceful unimplemented feature handling

## Architecture Considerations
- Use Go's `time.ParseDuration` for flexible timeout parsing
- Define `RobotResponse` base struct for all JSON outputs
- Create `resolveAgentType()` helper for consistent normalization
- Define `error_code` enum for programmatic error handling

## Testing Strategy
- Unit tests for each fix
- Integration tests for common robot workflows
- Validate JSON against documented schema","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-15T15:53:32.92907-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-15T17:53:47.007992-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-15T17:53:47.007992-05:00","close_reason":"All epic beads complete: R1 (duration strings), R2 (standardized errors), R3 (type aliasing), R4 (unimplemented handling)"}

 {"id":"ntm-161","title":"Implement ntm mail inbox command","description":"# Implement ntm mail inbox Command

## Purpose

Display an AGGREGATE inbox view showing messages across ALL agents in the project, not just NTM's coordinator messages.

## IMPORTANT: Aggregate View

This is not just NTM's inbox. It shows:
1. Messages TO any agent in the project (GreenCastle, BlueLake, etc.)
2. Messages FROM any agent in the project
3. Filtered by session agents if desired

This gives the user visibility into all agent-to-agent communication.

## Command Syntax

```bash
# Show all messages in project (across all agents)
ntm mail inbox myproject

# Filter to session's agents only
ntm mail inbox myproject --session-agents

# Filter to specific agent
ntm mail inbox myproject --agent GreenCastle

# Urgent only
ntm mail inbox myproject --urgent

# JSON for scripts
ntm mail inbox myproject --json
```

## Output Format

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Project Inbox: myproject (5 agents)                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ● #42 [URGENT] API Review Needed                            │
│   GreenCastle → BlueLake                                    │
│   2 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123 │ ⚠️ Ack required         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #41 Tests passing, ready for merge                        │
│   RedStone → GreenCastle, BlueLake                          │
│   15 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #40 Starting work on src/api/**                           │
│   ntm_myproject → all                                       │
│   30 minutes ago │ (coordinator announcement)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Showing messages involving: GreenCastle, BlueLake, RedStone, ntm_myproject
```

## Implementation

1. Discover all agents in project via Agent Mail
2. Fetch inbox for EACH agent
3. Deduplicate (same message may appear in multiple inboxes)
4. Sort by timestamp
5. Display with sender → recipient format

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Shows messages across ALL project agents
- [ ] Deduplication (message shown once even if multiple recipients)
- [ ] Filter by --session-agents, --agent, --urgent
- [ ] Clear indication of sender → recipient
- [ ] Coordinator messages distinguished from agent messages
- [ ] --json output for scripts","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ChartreuseLake","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:48.350455-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T21:21:36.691718-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T21:21:36.691718-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-161","depends_on_id":"ntm-yxa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:55.9288-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-3n9","title":"Implement agent type detection from pane content","description":"Improve agent type detection beyond pane title parsing.

## Current State
detectAgentType() in robot.go only checks pane title. This is unreliable because:
- Pane titles may not be set
- Users may use custom titles
- Agent type may not be in title

## Improved Detection
1. **Pane Title**: Check for 'claude', 'codex', 'gemini', etc.
2. **Process Name**: Check running process (claude, codex-cli, etc.)
3. **Pane Content**: Look for characteristic prompts/output:
   - Claude: '>' prompt, 'Claude' mentions
   - Codex: 'codex>' prompt, OpenAI references
   - Gemini: Gemini-specific formatting
   - Cursor: Cursor AI patterns
   - Windsurf: Windsurf patterns
   - Aider: 'aider>' prompt
4. **Environment**: Check for agent-specific env vars

## Detection Priority
Process name > Pane content > Pane title > Unknown

## Caching
Cache detected type per pane to avoid repeated content parsing. Invalidate on session reload.

## Output
```go
type AgentDetection struct {
    Type       string  // claude, codex, etc.
    Confidence float64 // 0.0-1.0
    Method     string  // how detected: process, content, title
}
```","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:53:43.118071-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:15.946452-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:15.946452-05:00","comments":[{"id":11,"issue_id":"ntm-3n9","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Detection Strategies

### Priority Order
1. **Process-based** (highest confidence)
   - ps aux | grep for process names: claude, codex-cli, gemini-cli
   - Check /proc/{pid}/cmdline on Linux
   - Most reliable but requires shell execution

2. **Environment-based**
   - Check for CLAUDE_*, OPENAI_*, GOOGLE_* env vars in pane
   - Can infer agent type from env configuration

3. **Content-based**
   - Scan last N lines of pane output
   - Look for characteristic patterns:
     - Claude: '>' prompt, 'Claude' in output, tool use XML
     - Codex: 'codex>' prompt, OpenAI references
     - Gemini: 'gemini>' prompt
     - Aider: 'aider>' or '/help' in prompt
   
4. **Title-based** (lowest confidence)
   - Current implementation - fallback only

### Caching Strategy
```go
type AgentTypeCache struct {
    cache map[string]CachedDetection // key: session:pane
    ttl   time.Duration              // default 60s
    mu    sync.RWMutex
}

type CachedDetection struct {
    Type       string
    Confidence float64
    Method     string
    DetectedAt time.Time
}
```

### Confidence Scores
- Process match: 0.95
- Env match: 0.85
- Content match: 0.70
- Title match: 0.50
- Unknown: 0.0","created_at":"2025-12-10T07:55:48Z"}]}
 {"id":"ntm-3ogq","title":"T6: Enhanced empty states with icons and guidance","description":"# T6: Enhanced Empty States with Icons and Guidance

## Parent Epic
[EPIC] Dashboard Visual Polish (ntm-af32)

## Problem Statement
Current empty states are minimal:
\`\`\`
No metrics yet
\`\`\`

This feels unfinished. Premium empty states:
- Include visual elements (icons)
- Explain WHY it's empty
- Guide users on what to do

## Solution
Create enhanced empty state component:

### Before
\`\`\`
No metrics yet
\`\`\`

### After
\`\`\`
         ◎
    No metrics yet
    
  Data will appear when
  agents start working
\`\`\`

## Visual Design

### Standard Empty State
\`\`\`
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                     │
│              ◎                      │
│        No metrics yet               │
│                                     │
│     Data appears when agents        │
│        start processing             │
│                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
\`\`\`

### Empty with Action
\`\`\`
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                     │
│              ○                      │
│       No command history            │
│                                     │
│    Send a prompt with 's' key       │
│     to start building history       │
│                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
\`\`\`

### Empty with External Cause
\`\`\`
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                     │
│              ◇                      │
│     Beads not initialized           │
│                                     │
│      Run 'bd init' in your          │
│     project to enable tracking      │
│                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
\`\`\`

## Implementation Details

### Component API
\`\`\`go
type EmptyStateOptions struct {
    Icon        string  // Visual icon (◎, ○, ◇, etc.)
    Title       string  // Primary message
    Description string  // Explanatory text (optional)
    Action      string  // Suggested action (optional)
    Width       int     // Available width
    Centered    bool    // Center in container (default: true)
}

func RenderEmptyState(opts EmptyStateOptions) string {
    // Render centered, styled empty state
}
\`\`\`

### Icon Meanings
| Icon | Meaning | Use Case |
|------|---------|----------|
| ◎ | Waiting | Data not yet available |
| ○ | Empty | Checked, nothing found |
| ◇ | External | Needs external action |
| ✓ | Success | Empty is good (no errors) |
| ? | Unknown | Couldn't determine |

### Panel Updates
Each panel gets appropriate empty state:

| Panel | Icon | Title | Description |
|-------|------|-------|-------------|
| Metrics | ◎ | No metrics yet | Data appears when agents start |
| History | ○ | No command history | Send prompts to build history |
| Files | ○ | No recent changes | File changes will appear here |
| Beads | ◇ | Not initialized | Run 'bd init' to enable |
| CASS | ◎ | No context found | Relevant history will appear |
| Alerts | ✓ | All clear | No alerts to display |

### Files to Create/Modify
- \`internal/tui/components/state.go\` - Enhanced empty state
- \`internal/tui/dashboard/panels/*.go\` - Use new empty states

- [ ] EmptyStateOptions component created
- [ ] All panels use enhanced empty states
- [ ] Icons are appropriate for each context
- [ ] Descriptions are helpful (not generic)
- [ ] Centered layout looks balanced
- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear

2. Verify each panel shows appropriate empty state
3. Verify empty state disappears when data arrives

 {"id":"ntm-3vm7","title":"R3: Agent type aliasing across all commands","description":"# R3: Unify Agent Type Aliasing Across Robot Flags

## Parent Epic
[EPIC] Robot Command Reliability (ntm-0q9o)

## IMPORTANT: Significant Infrastructure Already Exists

### Already Implemented:
- \`detectAgentType(title)\` in robot.go - detects from pane title
- \`DetectAgentTypeEnhanced()\` in detection.go - multi-method detection
- \`agentTypeString()\` in robot.go - converts tmux.AgentType to string
- \`AgentBadge(agentType)\` in styles/badges.go - handles cc/claude/codex/cod/gmi/gemini
- \`AgentBorderColor(agentType)\` in layout.go - handles agent colors
- \`AgentType\` enum in cli/agent_spec.go - defines AgentTypeClaude, AgentTypeCodex, AgentTypeGemini

### The Actual Gap:
Robot flag \`--type\` filter may not accept all alias forms consistently.

## Problem Statement
While most aliasing works internally, the \`--type\` flag for robot commands may not accept all forms:
\`\`\`bash
# Do all these work?
ntm --robot-send=test --type=claude --msg="hi"  # long form
ntm --robot-send=test --type=cc --msg="hi"      # short form
ntm --robot-send=test --type=claude_code --msg="hi"  # underscore form
\`\`\`

## Solution
Ensure \`--type\` flag uses centralized resolution:

### Step 1: Verify/Create Unified Resolver
Check if a single \`resolveAgentType()\` function exists. If not, create one that:
\`\`\`go
// ResolveAgentType normalizes any agent type string to canonical form.
func ResolveAgentType(input string) string {
    normalized := strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(input))
    normalized = strings.ReplaceAll(normalized, "-", "_")
    
    switch normalized {
    case "cc", "claude", "claude_code":
        return "claude"
    case "cod", "codex", "codex_cli":
        return "codex"
    case "gmi", "gemini", "gemini_cli":
        return "gemini"
    // ... other types
    default:
        return normalized // Unknown type passed through
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Step 2: Use in Robot Flag Parsing
In root.go where \`--type\` is parsed:
\`\`\`go
if robotSendType != "" {
    agentTypes = strings.Split(robotSendType, ",")
    for i, t := range agentTypes {
        agentTypes[i] = ResolveAgentType(t)  // Normalize
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Files to Audit/Modify
- \`internal/robot/robot.go\` - Check type filtering logic
- \`internal/cli/root.go\` - --type flag parsing
- May need to create shared resolver if doesn't exist

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] \`--type=cc\` works same as \`--type=claude\`
- [ ] \`--type=cod\` works same as \`--type=codex\`
- [ ] \`--type=gmi\` works same as \`--type=gemini\`
- [ ] JSON output always uses canonical form
- [ ] No new enums/types created (use existing)

## Testing Notes
\`\`\`bash
# Test all alias forms work:
ntm --robot-send=test --type=cc --msg="hi"
ntm --robot-send=test --type=claude --msg="hi"
ntm --robot-send=test --type=claude_code --msg="hi"
# All should target the same panes
\`\`\`

## Integration Note for AI Agents
The canonical forms returned in JSON output are: \`claude\`, \`codex\`, \`gemini\`, \`cursor\`, \`windsurf\`, \`aider\`, \`user\`, \`unknown\`. Agents should use these forms when setting filters, though short forms will work.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-15T15:54:38.131535-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-15T17:52:41.844139-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-15T17:52:41.844139-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented unified agent type aliasing with ResolveAgentType function and detectAgentType short form support"}

 {"id":"ntm-9f8k","title":"Implement restart strategy (terminate, browser switch, restart agent)","description":"# Restart Strategy Implementation

Strategy B: Quit session, switch browser account, start fresh.

## Why This is Often Better
- Near compaction: context is getting long anyway
- After compaction: minimal context to preserve
- New sessions auto-use the active browser account!

## Flow
```go
func (o *Orchestrator) executeRestartStrategy(paneID string, provider string, targetAccount *accounts.AccountState) error {
    flow := o.authFlows[provider]
    
    // 1. Terminate existing session gracefully
    log.Printf("Terminating session in pane %s", paneID)
    if err := flow.TerminateSession(paneID); err != nil {
        // Fallback: force kill
        tmux.SendInterrupt(paneID)
        time.Sleep(time.Second)
        tmux.SendInterrupt(paneID)
        time.Sleep(time.Second)
        tmux.SendKeys(paneID, "exit", true)
    }
    
    // 2. Wait for shell prompt
    if err := o.waitForShellPrompt(paneID, 10*time.Second); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("session did not terminate: %w", err)
    }
    
    // 3. Prompt user for browser auth
    o.promptBrowserAuth(targetAccount)
    
    // 4. Wait for user confirmation
    fmt.Println("Press ENTER when you have logged into", targetAccount.Email)
    bufio.NewReader(os.Stdin).ReadLine()
    
    // 5. Start new agent session
    projectDir := o.getProjectDir(paneID)
    agentCmd := o.cfg.GetAgentCommand(provider)
    fullCmd := fmt.Sprintf("cd %q && %s", projectDir, agentCmd)
    
    return tmux.SendKeys(paneID, fullCmd, true)
}
```

## Shell Prompt Detection
```go
func (o *Orchestrator) waitForShellPrompt(paneID string, timeout time.Duration) error {
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), timeout)
    defer cancel()
    
    promptPatterns := []string{
        `\$\s*$`,   // bash prompt
        `%\s*$`,    // zsh prompt
        `>\s*$`,    // generic prompt
    }
    
    ticker := time.NewTicker(500 * time.Millisecond)
    defer ticker.Stop()
    
    for {
        select {
        case <-ctx.Done():
            return ctx.Err()
        case <-ticker.C:
            output, _ := tmux.CapturePaneOutput(paneID, 5)
            for _, pattern := range promptPatterns {
                if regexp.MustCompile(pattern).MatchString(output) {
                    return nil
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

## Storage
```go
type CommandEntry struct {
    ID        uint64
    Session   string
    Pane      string
    Command   string
    SentAt    time.Time
    AckAt     *time.Time       // when agent acked
    OutputLen int              // lines of output produced

}

```bash

}
```

## Persistence
Store in SQLite DB per session:

 {"id":"ntm-gcv","title":"Implement notification system with desktop and webhook support","description":"Create a notification system that can alert users via multiple channels.

## What This Task Does

Build infrastructure to send notifications when events occur:
- Desktop notifications (native OS)
- Webhook POST (Slack, Discord, custom)
- Shell command execution
- Log file appending

## Notification Events

- agent.error - Agent hit error state
- agent.crashed - Agent process exited
- agent.restarted - Agent was auto-restarted
- agent.idle - Agent waiting for input
- session.created - New session spawned
- session.killed - Session terminated
- health.degraded - Overall health dropped

## Configuration

[notifications]
enabled = true
events = ["agent.error", "agent.crashed", "agent.restarted"]

[notifications.desktop]
enabled = true

[notifications.webhook]
url = "https://hooks.slack.com/services/XXX/YYY/ZZZ"
# Payload template (Go template syntax)
template = '''
{"text": "NTM: {{.Event}} - {{.Message}}"}
'''

[notifications.shell]
command = "~/.config/ntm/notify.sh"
pass_json = true

[notifications.log]
path = "~/.local/share/ntm/notifications.jsonl"

## Implementation

Create internal/notify package:

type Event struct {
    Type      string
    Session   string
    Agent     string
    Message   string
    Timestamp time.Time
    Data      map[string]interface{}
}

type Notifier interface {
    Notify(event Event) error
}

Implementations:
- DesktopNotifier: osascript (macOS), notify-send (Linux)
- WebhookNotifier: HTTP POST with template rendering
- ShellNotifier: exec.Command with JSON stdin
- LogNotifier: Append to JSONL file

## Desktop Notifications

macOS:
osascript -e 'display notification "message" with title "NTM"'

Linux (notify-send):
notify-send "NTM" "message"

Windows/WSL:
powershell.exe -Command "[Windows.UI.Notifications...]"

## ntm notify command

Manual notification for testing/scripting:

$ ntm notify "Build complete" --session myproject
$ ntm notify --event agent.error --message "Rate limit hit"

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Desktop notifications work on macOS and Linux
- [ ] Webhook POST sends correctly formatted payload
- [ ] Shell command receives event as JSON
- [ ] Events can be filtered in config
- [ ] ntm notify command for manual triggers
- [ ] Errors in notification don't break NTM

 {"id":"ntm-ijua","title":"Integrate bv graph analysis with scan findings","description":"## Overview
Connect UBS scan findings with beads_viewer (bv) graph analysis to surface insights about how code quality issues relate to task dependencies.

## Background
bv provides powerful graph analysis of beads. When scan findings become beads, we can:
- See which critical issues block the most work
- Identify quality hotspots via graph centrality
- Prioritize fixes by downstream impact

## Implementation Details

### Integration Points

1. **Finding Impact Analysis**
   - When a finding becomes a bead, analyze its position in dependency graph
   - Surface findings that block other work
   - `ntm scan --analyze-impact` shows findings sorted by graph impact

2. **Quality Hotspot Detection**  
   - Files with many findings that are central to the codebase
   - Use bv's centrality metrics on file-based grouping
   - `ntm scan --hotspots` shows most impactful files

3. **Fix Prioritization**
   - Combine severity + graph centrality for smart priority
   - Critical issues blocking 5 tasks > critical issues blocking 0
   - `ntm scan --priority-report` generates actionable list

### Output Format
```
📊 Scan Impact Analysis

High-Impact Findings (by downstream blockers):
  1. [P0] SQL injection in auth.go:45 - blocks 3 tasks
  2. [P1] Unhandled error in api.go:89 - blocks 2 tasks
  
Quality Hotspots:
  1. internal/auth/login.go - 5 findings, centrality: 0.82
  2. internal/api/handler.go - 3 findings, centrality: 0.71

Recommended Fix Order:
  1. auth.go:45 (impact score: 9.5)
  2. api.go:89 (impact score: 7.2)
  ...
```

### Files to Create/Modify
- internal/scanner/analysis.go - Graph analysis integration
- internal/scanner/priority.go - Smart prioritization
- internal/cli/scan.go - Add --analyze-impact, --hotspots flags

## Dependencies
- Requires beads bridge (ntm-dv1c) to create beads from findings
- Uses bv CLI or library for graph metrics

- [ ] Hotspots correctly identified
- [ ] Priority combines severity and graph position

 {"id":"ntm-iws","title":"Implement --persona flag for spawn/add","description":"# Implement --persona Flag for spawn/add

## Purpose

Allow spawning agents using persona definitions.

## Syntax

```bash
ntm spawn myproject --persona=architect --persona=implementer:2
ntm add myproject --persona=reviewer
```

## Flag Parsing

```go
type PersonaSpec struct {
    Name  string
    Count int  // Default 1
}

func ParsePersonaSpec(value string) PersonaSpec {
    parts := strings.SplitN(value, ":", 2)
    spec := PersonaSpec{Name: parts[0], Count: 1}
    if len(parts) > 1 {
        spec.Count, _ = strconv.Atoi(parts[1])
    }
    return spec
}
```

## Persona Resolution

```go
func ResolvePersona(name string) (*Persona, error) {
    // 1. Check project personas (.ntm/personas.toml)
    // 2. Check user personas (~/.config/ntm/personas.toml)
    // 3. Check built-in personas
    // 4. Return error if not found
}
```

## Spawn Integration

When spawning with --persona:
1. Resolve persona definition
2. Map agent_type to spawn flag (--cc, --cod, --gmi)

 {"id":"ntm-jzkc","title":"T1: Responsive help bar using RenderHelpBar component","description":"# T1: Responsive Help Bar Using RenderHelpBar Component

## Parent Epic
[EPIC] Dashboard Discoverability (ntm-wb8v)

## Problem Statement
The dashboard has a custom help bar implementation that doesn't handle narrow widths:

\`\`\`go
// dashboard.go:2326 - Custom implementation
func (m Model) renderHelpBar() string {
    items := []struct{ key, desc string }{
        {"↑↓", "navigate"},
        // ... hardcoded items
    }
    // ... custom rendering (no width handling)
}
\`\`\`

Meanwhile, \`components/help.go\` has a reusable component with progressive truncation:

\`\`\`go
// Unused in dashboard!
func RenderHelpBar(opts HelpBarOptions) string {
    // Progressive truncation: removes hints from right until fits
    // Width-aware rendering
    // Consistent styling
}
\`\`\`

## Solution
Replace custom \`renderHelpBar()\` with \`components.RenderHelpBar()\`.

## Implementation Details

### Before
\`\`\`go
func (m Model) renderHelpBar() string {
    items := []struct{ key, desc string }{
        {"↑↓", "navigate"},
        {"1-9", "select"},
        {"z", "zoom"},
        // ...
    }
    // Custom rendering
}
\`\`\`

### After
\`\`\`go
func (m Model) renderHelpBar() string {
    return components.RenderHelpBar(components.HelpBarOptions{
        Hints: components.DefaultDashboardHints(),
        Width: m.width - 4,  // Account for margins
    })
}
\`\`\`

### Files to Modify
- \`internal/tui/dashboard/dashboard.go\` - Replace renderHelpBar implementation

 {"id":"ntm-ozcj.1","title":"CASS Infrastructure: Create internal/cass Go client package","description":"Create the foundational Go client package for CASS integration.

## Overview
This package provides a Go interface to CASS robot mode, enabling NTM to query past agent sessions, check health, and retrieve insights.

## Files to Create
- internal/cass/client.go - Main client with exec wrapper
- internal/cass/types.go - Response types (SearchHit, Status, Capabilities, etc.)
- internal/cass/search.go - Search API wrapper
- internal/cass/health.go - Health check integration
- internal/cass/timeline.go - Timeline API wrapper
- internal/cass/client_test.go - Unit tests with mocks

## Key Design Decisions

### Execution Model
Use os/exec to call cass CLI (not HTTP) because:
- CASS is a local tool, not a server
- Robot mode is designed for CLI consumption
- No need for daemon/socket infrastructure

### Error Handling
- CASS exit codes map to Go errors
- Retry on transient errors (timeout, busy)
- Graceful degradation when CASS not installed

### Response Types
Mirror CASS robot mode JSON schemas:
```go
